name: Smelting (Bronze Age)
tagline: Reducing copper and tin ores with carbon to produce castable bronze alloys
problem:
Native copper exists but is geologically rare compared with oxidised copper minerals. Humans needed a reliable path from colourful rocks to metal that could be hammered, cast, and resharpened. Bronze — typically copper alloyed with tin — extends hardness and lowers melting range compared with pure copper, improving casting yield and edge performance for tools and weapons.

The obstacle is chemistry: metal oxides do not melt into metal in open wood fires. You need a reducing atmosphere where carbon monoxide strips oxygen from ore, and you need temperatures high enough for slag formation and metal pooling, held in refractory vessels.

Bronze also created long-distance trade incentives: tin is scarce relative to copper in many regions, linking economies across seas. That social coupling is as much part of the technology as furnaces.
overview:
Copper smelting begins with roasting sulphide ores if present — driving off sulphur as oxides — then charging oxide ores with charcoal in a shaft or bowl furnace with forced air (bellows or natural draft). Carbon burns to CO, which reduces CuO to Cu and CO2. Iron-rich gangue fluxes with silica to form slag that floats on metal.

Tin can be smelted similarly and alloyed, or cassiterite may be reduced directly in the copper melt. Typical bronze might range roughly 5–12% tin by weight depending on desired properties — higher tin increases hardness but brittleness.

Casting uses piece-moulds of clay or stone, later lost-wax for complexity. Work-hardening of hammered bronze increases surface hardness; annealing resets grain structure for further shaping.

Safety and materials limits matter: arsenical copper phases exist in early metal — toxic fumes from roasting require ventilation. Furnace refractories fail if slag is too fluid or iron content too high for a copper slag system.

principles.name: Reduction potential
principles.explanation: Carbon monoxide is the primary gaseous reductant in charcoal furnaces; maintaining reducing conditions near the charge is essential while still supplying heat.

principles.name: Slag chemistry
principles.explanation: Impurities combine into a liquid silicate slag with the right basicity; slag captures iron and gangue so metal pools separately.

principles.name: Alloy solidification
principles.explanation: Tin lowers liquidus temperature and changes microstructure on cooling — controlling composition tunes hardness versus toughness.

principles.name: Work hardening
principles.explanation: Plastic deformation increases dislocation density, hardening bronze until heat allows recrystallisation.

components.name: Shaft or bowl furnace
components.function: Contains charge, concentrates heat, routes slag and metal
components.madeFrom: Clay lining, tuyère ports for air
components.criticalNote: Tuyère angle and depth set reaction zone location

components.name: Bellows or blowers
components.function: Raises temperature by increasing oxygen supply to charcoal
components.criticalNote: Intermittent blasting risks thermal cycling cracks — design refractory thickness accordingly

components.name: Crucible (optional stage)
components.function: Collects metal for alloying and pouring
components.position: Inserted or used as cupola in some traditions
components.madeFrom: Fired clay graphite-tempered in later periods
components.criticalNote: Thermal shock from cold tools can fracture hot crucibles

components.name: Mould system
components.criticalNote: Venting prevents gas traps that make porous useless castings

components.name: Use-case boundary
components.function: Defines what Smelting (Bronze Age) is meant to accomplish and what it must not be used for
components.madeFrom: Written requirements, hazard notes, local constraints, and responsible operator assignment
components.criticalNote: A vague use case leads to unsafe improvisation.

rawMaterials.name: Copper ore (malachite, chalcopyrite after roasting)
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Metallic asteroids for native metals — different process chain
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Crushing, roasting sulphides, beneficiation by hand sorting

rawMaterials.name: Tin ore (cassiterite)
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Rare in homogeneous form — substitution with arsenic historically (toxic) avoided in modern ethics
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Washing placer gravels; reduction in furnace

rawMaterials.name: Charcoal
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Electrolytic copper wins before charcoal logistics replicate off-world
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Sized lumps for consistent bed permeability

buildSteps.title: Roast sulphide ores if needed
buildSteps.description: Open roast beds drive off sulphur as SO2 — extremely hazardous without understanding plume direction. Oxide ores may skip roasting.
buildSteps.warningNote: SO2 damages lungs; arsenic-bearing ores are deadly — avoid unknown ores

buildSteps.title: Build furnace and preheat
buildSteps.description: Dry out clay lining slowly. Establish charcoal bed with steady blast until refractory is hot.
buildSteps.warningNote: Water in lining flashes to steam — explosive spalling

buildSteps.title: Charge ore and charcoal in layers
buildSteps.description: Alternate to maintain permeability. Watch for colour of flame and slag drip — experience-guided.
buildSteps.warningNote: Metal drip can explode on wet ground

buildSteps.title: Tap metal and refine
buildSteps.description: Let slag run off; collect copper metal into crucible for tin addition. Skim oxide skins.
buildSteps.warningNote: Molten metal splash causes severe burns — leather aprons insufficient; prefer full coverage plan

buildSteps.title: Cast or forge
buildSteps.description: Pour into moulds or hammer while hot. Anneal when cracks appear from overwork.
buildSteps.warningNote: Hot shortness varies by composition — test small samples

history.event: Smelted copper and early bronzes appear across multiple Old World centres — convergent innovation versus diffusion remains debated.
Long-distance tin trade links mark Bronze Age economies.

impact:
Bronze weaponry and tools shifted military and agricultural gradients where tin supply allowed. Societies invested in mining camps, harbour infrastructure, and craft guild-like specialisation.

Bronze is also a cognitive milestone: humans learned to see rocks as chemical feedstock. That worldview is prerequisite to iron, steel, and modern materials.

sources.title: UNESCO — Lists of Intangible Cultural Heritage
sources.note: Documentation model for traditional craft and metallurgical heritage

sources.title: NIST — Materials Measurement Science portal
sources.note: Materials characterization methods relevant to alloy analysis
